region_of_origin: Japan (dish adapted into Thai and pan-Asian seafood restaurant menus in Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a yellowtail food product class (FOODON_03301116) but no specific entry for grilled amberjack collar. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers Seriola spp. (amberjack/yellowtail) as a species group. No specific collar-cut or grilled preparation code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for hamachi amberjack collar as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of 2 hamachi amberjack collars weighs approximately 300 g in total, representing the pectoral-fin collar section of the fish.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, yellowtail, mixed species, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 15230)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw yellowtail (Seriola spp.) used as the closest approved-source match. The collar cut is the fattiest section of the fish; values are adapted upward for fat and energy relative to the whole-fish average to reflect the collar's higher fat content, consistent with published literature on fish collar composition. Grilling causes minor moisture loss; per-100g values are adjusted to reflect cooked weight. Protein and micronutrient values are held close to the raw reference. This is an estimated adaptation; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Pure Protein and Fat Foods — University of Sydney GI Database reference framework
gi_source.source_match_notes: Hamachi amberjack collar contains no digestible carbohydrates (carbohydrate_g = 0.0 confirmed zero for this protein-and-fat fish food). Pure protein and fat foods have a glycemic index effectively equal to zero. Per the Veritas zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule convention for non-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) and gi_category is set to low. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate content is zero. This is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database guidance that protein- and fat-dominant foods do not raise blood glucose and are assigned GI values near zero.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~65 mg per 100 g); rich in omega-3 fatty acids which support vascular health
hypertension.guidance: Hamachi collar is relatively low in sodium in its plain grilled form; individuals with hypertension should avoid added salt or high-sodium dipping sauces and can include this dish as part of a heart-healthy, low-sodium meal plan.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero digestible carbohydrates; high-quality protein and omega-3 fatty acids support glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled hamachi collar contains no carbohydrates and has a glycemic index effectively of zero, making it an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control of accompanying sauces or rice is advised to manage overall glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA) with moderate saturated fat; cholesterol ~62 mg per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high omega-3 content of hamachi collar (approximately 3.8 g polyunsaturated fat per 100 g) is associated with reduced triglycerides and anti-inflammatory effects; individuals with cardiovascular disease can consume this dish in moderate portions as part of a Mediterranean-style or heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hamachi Amberjack Collar (2 Pieces) is a premium Japanese-origin seafood dish featuring the pectoral-fin collar section (kama) of the hamachi, also known as Japanese amberjack or yellowtail (Seriola quinqueradiata). The collar is the fattiest, most flavourful cut of the fish, located just behind the head and pectoral fins. It is typically seasoned with salt and grilled or broiled at high heat until the skin is charred and crispy while the interior flesh remains moist, rich, and tender. The dish is widely served in Japanese restaurants and has been adopted into pan-Asian and Thai seafood menus in Singapore, including at establishments such as Shrimp Prawn Seafood at Boat Quay. Nutritionally, hamachi collar is an excellent source of high-quality complete protein (~20.5 g per 100 g), healthy fats including omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA, ~3.8 g per 100 g), vitamin D (~5.1 mcg per 100 g), vitamin B12 (~2.8 mcg per 100 g), selenium (~36.5 mcg per 100 g), and niacin (~7.5 mg per 100 g). It contains zero digestible carbohydrates, giving it a glycemic index effectively of zero, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic-friendly diets. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. A typical restaurant serving of 2 collars weighs approximately 300 g. The rich umami flavour, high fat content, and crispy-charred skin make this a prized dish among seafood enthusiasts. Clinically, the omega-3 content supports cardiovascular health, the zero-carbohydrate profile benefits glycaemic management in Type 2 Diabetes, and the moderate sodium content makes it acceptable for most hypertension management plans when consumed without high-sodium condiments.
common_user_queries: Is hamachi collar good for diabetics?
Is fish collar high in fat?

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry exists for grilled hamachi amberjack collar specifically. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw yellowtail (Seriola spp., NDB 15230), with upward adjustment for fat and energy to reflect the collar cut's higher fat content relative to whole-fish averages, and minor adjustment for moisture loss during grilling. GI is set to 1 (sentinel for effectively zero) as the food contains no digestible carbohydrates. Confidence is medium rather than high because the collar-specific adaptation is estimated rather than directly measured.
notes: 1. Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero: hamachi (yellowtail) fish collar contains no digestible carbohydrates by nature; this is a protein-and-fat food with naturally zero carbohydrate content. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as fish contains no plant fibre. 2. Trans_fat_g is 0.0; yellowtail does not contain industrially produced trans fats; any trace geometric isomers from marine sources are below detection threshold (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy). 3. Glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is low per convention for protein-and-fat dominant foods. Glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is zero. 4. The cuisine tag in the input JSON is listed as Thai, reflecting the restaurant context (Shrimp Prawn Seafood, Boat Quay, Singapore), though the dish is of Japanese origin. Both contexts are preserved. 5. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local_names are provided as descriptive phrases or romanized forms to meet the Singaporean multilingual requirements, as direct native names for this specific Japanese dish are not commonly established in these languages in Singapore. 6. Sodium value of 65 mg per 100 g reflects plain grilled preparation without added sauces; restaurant preparations may add salt or serve with soy-based dipping sauces that would increase sodium significantly. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, and micronutrients are estimated from USDA SR Legacy yellowtail data and are not independently verified for the collar cut specifically. 8. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301116 is used as closest parent (yellowtail food product); no exact collar-cut grilled entry exists in FoodOn. FoodEx2 code A00LC covers Seriola spp. as closest parent. SNOMED CT has no applicable food concept for this item.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full record from USDA SR Legacy yellowtail data adapted for collar cut. Set carbohydrate_g=0.0 with zero justification. Set GI=1 sentinel with documentation. Added Thai local name per cuisine requirement. Added Japanese local name per dish origin. Added Chinese names. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases.

Iteration 2: Addressed REJECT from previous iteration. Interpreted validator feedback on G6 (multilingual naming compliance) to mean Singaporean dish rules apply due to 'field survey data' source. Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi descriptive names to `local_names` and `aliases_by_language`. Added all new aliases and the previously missing 'Kho pla hamachi (2 chin)' to the flat `aliases` list to resolve Q8 consistency. Updated notes to reflect multilingual strategy. Re-verified all hard gates and Q1-Q8 criteria.
